flashcards.front: What is a List in Data Structures?
flashcards.back: A list is a linear data structure that stores an ordered collection of elements. Unlike arrays, lists support dynamic memory allocation, allowing efficient insertion and deletion without fixed size constraints.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between an Array and a Linked List?
flashcards.back: Arrays have fixed size and store elements in contiguous memory locations. Linked lists use nodes with data and pointer fields, allowing non-contiguous storage and dynamic sizing.

flashcards.front: What are the three main types of Linked Lists?
flashcards.back: Singly Linked List (unidirectional traversal), Doubly Linked List (bidirectional with prev and next pointers), and Circular Linked List (last node points back to first).

flashcards.front: What is the structure of a node in a Singly Linked List?
flashcards.back: Each node contains two fields: DATA (stores the actual element) and NEXT (pointer/reference to the next node in the list).

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of inserting a node at the beginning of a Linked List?
flashcards.back: O(1) - Constant time, as it only requires updating the head pointer to point to the new node.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of inserting a node at the end of a Linked List?
flashcards.back: O(n) - Linear time, because we must traverse the entire list to find the last node (unless a tail pointer is maintained).

flashcards.front: How does deletion work in a Linked List?
flashcards.back: To delete a node, adjust the pointer of the previous node to skip the node being deleted. For first node, update head; for middle nodes, link previous to next; for last node, find second-last and set its next to NULL.

flashcards.front: What is traversal in a Linked List?
flashcards.back: Traversal is the process of visiting each node in the list exactly once, typically using a pointer that moves from node to node until NULL is reached.

flashcards.front: What is a Doubly Linked List?
flashcards.back: A doubly linked list has nodes with three fields: DATA (element), PREV (pointer to previous node), and NEXT (pointer to next node), allowing traversal in both directions.

flashcards.front: What is a Circular Linked List?
flashcards.back: In a circular linked list, the last node's NEXT pointer points back to the first node (head), forming a closed loop. It can be singly or doubly circular.

flashcards.front: What are the advantages of Linked Lists over Arrays?
flashcards.back: Dynamic size (no fixed capacity), efficient insertion/deletion O(1) at beginning, no memory wastage, and no need for contiguous memory.

flashcards.front: What are the disadvantages of Linked Lists?
flashcards.back: Higher memory usage (extra pointer space), no random access (O(n) to reach nth element), complex implementation, and not cache-friendly.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of searching in a Linked List?
flashcards.back: O(n) - Linear time complexity, as we may need to traverse all nodes in worst case to find the element.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Singly and Doubly Linked Lists?
flashcards.back: Singly lists use less memory but allow traversal in one direction. Doubly lists use more memory (extra prev pointer) but allow bidirectional traversal and easier deletion of previous nodes.

flashcards.front: What is a header node in a Linked List?
flashcards.back: A header node is a dummy node placed at the beginning of the list that contains no actual data but simplifies insertion and deletion operations at the start of the list.
